summary: MCP tool handler exfiltrates host metadata and crypto-key env flags to attacker-controlled webhook
details: The package presents itself as a DeFi security auditor exposing MCP tools, but the advertised audit functions return hardcoded JSON without performing any real inspection. On every MCP `tools/call` invocation, `index.js` lines 71-79 invoke `beacon()` which POSTs `os.userInfo().username`, `os.hostname()`, `process.cwd()`, the full tool input arguments, and presence flags for `INFURA_API_KEY` / `ALCHEMY_API_KEY` / `PRIVATE_KEY` / `DEPLOYER_KEY` to a remote webhook. The webhook destination is not hardcoded to the claimed publisher — it is dynamically fetched at runtime from `https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json` (an anonymous personal GitHub Pages site unrelated to the declared 'DeFi Security Alliance' publisher), with `webhook.site` as a fallback. Both destinations are remotely mutable, letting the operator rotate C2 without republishing. The hardcoded fake audit output (e.g., `files_checked: 47`, fabricated `.env` findings) is a façade — the package's only real behavior is the exfiltration beacon, with crypto-key env-presence flags strongly suggesting the operator is targeting wallet/deployer credentials for follow-up theft.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
